ThingDef.label: M15 Diskushandgranate
ThingDef.description: A German World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: M15 Kugelhandgranate
ThingDef.description: A German World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: M15 Stielhandgranate
ThingDef.description: An iconic German World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: M16 Stielhandgranate
ThingDef.description: An iconic German World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: M17 Eierhandgranate
ThingDef.description: A German World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: M17 Stielhandgranate
ThingDef.description: An iconic German World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: Rohrhandgranate Neu
ThingDef.description: An Austro-Hungarian World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: Rohrhandgranate Alt
ThingDef.description: An Austro-Hungarian World War I-era frag grenade.

ThingDef.label: Borchardt C93 Machine Pistol
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 7.65x25mm Borchardt. 8-round detachable mag. Select-Fire. The Borchardt C93 is a semi-automatic handgun designed by Hugo Borchardt in the early 1890s, using a unique toggle-lock design. About 3,000 Borchardt pistols were produced, making it the first successful semi-auto pistol ever put in production - however, it had no significant non-commercial sales, and any military use of the C93 was limited to experiments or by officers with privately procured sidearms. Nevertheless, the pistol was extremely influential on the development of future handguns; while working at Deutsche Waffen und Munitionsfabriken (DWM), Georg Luger was tasked with selling C93 pistols abroad. Receiving an extensive list of complaints, Luger was tasked to redesign the pistol, with the final design evolving into the iconic Luger pistol. Furthermore, the C93's 7.65mm cartridge was used as the basis for the 7.63x25mm cartridge developed for Mauser's C96 pistol. This is a C93 pistol that was tested in factory circa 1895 with an automatic fire capability, produced by making alterations to isolate the disconnector and allowing the weapon to expend all cartridges with a single trigger pull. The resulting pistol reportedly fired at approximately 1,340 rounds per minute and was quite unwieldy. The actual prototype is not known to have survived to the present, but it would be one of the first (if not THE first) proper "machine pistols" ever made.

ThingDef.label: Mauser C96 8mm Gasser
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 8mm Gasser. 10-round internal mag. Semi-Automatic. The Mauser C96 is a semi-automatic handgun designed by Fidel, Friedrich, and Josef Feederle in the 1890s. Produced initially by Mauser (and given its name by Paul Mauser himself) and later copied by many other entities, the C96 is iconic for being among the first widespread commercial semi-automatic handguns. Despite being acquired or tested in some form by almost every developed country at one point or another, the C96 pistol was never formally adopted by any military force; nevertheless, the C96 saw widespread use in military conflicts for over a half-century, and was also reasonably successful in private sales. This C96 is a rare variant from an Austro-Hungarian military contract and is chambered in 8mm Gasser.

ThingDef.label: Luger P08
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 9x19mm Parabellum. 8-round detachable mag. Semi-Automatic. The Luger is an iconic series of handguns designed by Georg Luger in the late 19th century. The Luger uses a toggle-lock short-recoil system originally derived from Hugo Borchardt's C93 pistol, which Georg Luger was tasked with improving upon in the mid-1890s while working at Deutsche Waffen- und Munitionsfabriken (DWM); the end result of these developments was the Parabellum pistol. The Luger proved to be successful with both military and commercial contracts, and was manufactured for over a half-century. This is a Pistole Modell 1908 (P08) Luger that was adopted by the German Army in 1908. The P08 had a number of small improvements on earlier models, abandoning the grip safety and shortening the barrel. P08 pistols continued to be used by the German military until the end of World War II.

ThingDef.label: Mauser M1917 Trench Carbine
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 9x19mm Parabellum. 40-round detachable mag. Semi-Automatic. The Mauser C96 is a semi-automatic handgun designed by Fidel, Friedrich, and Josef Feederle in the 1890s. Produced initially by Mauser (and given its name by Paul Mauser himself) and later copied by many other entities, the C96 is iconic for being among the first widespread commercial semi-automatic handguns. Despite being acquired or tested in some form by almost every developed country at one point or another, the C96 pistol was never formally adopted by any military force; nevertheless, the C96 saw widespread use in military conflicts for over a half-century, and was also reasonably successful in private sales. This M1917 "Trench Carbine" variant was a WW1-era resurrection of the mostly unsuccessful cavalry carbine concept from early in the C96's production, but with a number of adaptations. The M1917 featured a significantly shorter barrel and handguard than the cavalry carbine variants, and also fed from a 40-round detachable box magazine rather than stripper clips (similar to the future M712 Schnellfeuer.) As its name suggests, the M1917 was designed to be used for trench storming; however, automatic weapons like the MP18 that were already in testing were found to be more effective, and the M1917 was rejected by the army.

ThingDef.label: Carcano M91 Austrian
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 6.5x54mm Mannlicher. 6-round internal mag. Bolt-Action. The Carcano Modello 91 (M91) is an Italian bolt-action rifle developed in the 1890s by Salvatore Carcano to replace the Vetterli-Vitali rifles then in Italian service. The design of the Carcano includes both elements of Mannlicher and Mauser rifles, and uses a magazine design derived from the German Gewehr 1888 rifle. This rifle is one of approximately 49,500 that were captured by the Austro-Hungarian military during World War I and converted to fire the 6.5x54mm Mannlicher-Schönauer cartridge.

ThingDef.label: Berdan Model 1870 AZF
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 8x50mmR Mannlicher. 1-round chamber. Bolt-Action. This is a Russian Berdan II rifle that was captured by Austro-Hungarian forces during the First World War. Captured rifles were frequently marked with "AZF" (Artillerie Zeugs Fabrik) or "OEWG" (Osterreichische Waffenfabrik Gesellschaft.) While reissued rifles were initially used in the original 10.67x58mm caliber, many (including this rifle) were later rechambered in 8x50mmR Mannlicher as stocks of captured Russian ammunition began to dry up.

ThingDef.label: Mosin-Nagant 1907 Carbine AZF
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 8x50mmR Mannlicher. 5-round internal mag. Bolt-Action. The Mosin-Nagant, also known as the "three-line rifle," is a Russian bolt-action rifle designed by Sergei Mosin. The rifle was designed in the late 1880s, in response to the failures of the single-shot Berdan II rifle during the Russo-Turkish War, and Tsar Alexander III's desire for the Russian military to be equipped with rifles up to the standard of other European industrial nations. Mosin's design was submitted in 1889 for consideration along with two others, one by Émile Nagant and the other by a Captain Zinoviev. Mosin's rifle won out, and the final design selected for adoption was produced by 1891; this final rifle incorporated minor influences of Nagant's proposal, but the popular name "Mosin-Nagant" was never official. The Mosin platform was produced in military factories for almost 80 years, and saw military service even well into the 21st century. This is an M1907 carbine that was captured by Austro-Hungarian forces during the First World War. A number of Mosin rifles (including this one), marked with "AZF" (Artillerie Zeugs Fabrik), were converted to fire the 8x50mmR Mannlicher cartridge and reissued to troops.

ThingDef.label: Arisaka Type 30 Austrian
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 6.5x54mm Mannlicher. 5-round internal mag. Bolt-Action. The Type 30 Arisaka is a bolt-action rifle designed by the Koishikawa Arsenal at the behest of Arisaka Nariakira. Chambered for the 6.5x50mm Arisaka cartridge, the Type 30 (named for the 30th year of the Meiji period, 1897) replaced the Murata rifles that were previously in service. The Type 30 was replaced by the improved Type 38 rifle following the Russo-Japanese War, but Type 30 rifles continued to be used as a standard rifle through the end of World War I, and as a reservist rifle until the end of World War II. This rifle is an example that was procured by Russia during World War I, as part of batches received from both the United Kingdom and Japan itself. A number of these rifles (including this one) were captured by Austro-Hungarian forces on the eastern front and modified for reissue, being rechambered in 6.5x54mm Mannlicher-Schönauer and having their right sights replaced with those of the Mannlicher 1895 rifle.

ThingDef.label: Arisaka Type 38 Austrian
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 6.5x54mm Mannlicher. 5-round internal mag. Bolt-Action. The Type 38 Arisaka is a bolt-action rifle that was made to replace the previous Type 30 rifle, with production starting in 1905. Chambered for the 6.5x50mm Arisaka cartridge, the Type 38 improved on its predecessor by reducing the number of parts in the weapon and addressing the frequent reliability issues that were present. The Type 38 was the standard Japanese military rifle from 1905 all the way to 1939, when it was meant to be replaced by the 7.7mm Type 99 rifle; however, the Type 38 saw service and continued production until World War II ended. This rifle is an example that was procured by Russia during World War I, as part of batches received from Mexico, the United Kingdom, and Japan itself. A number of these rifles (including this one) were captured by Austro-Hungarian forces on the eastern front and rechambered in 6.5x54mm Mannlicher-Schönauer for reissue.
